docs.title: Welcome to k8s-inspector 2.0 🐦‍⬛
docs.text:
Not just monitoring — understanding. Not just alerts — answers. Not just problems — solutions.

k8s-inspector 2.0 is an intelligent, AI-powered observability platform for Kubernetes. It goes beyond traditional metrics by providing root cause analysis, cost optimization, security compliance checks, and automated remediation.

docs.title: Why k8s-inspector?
docs.text: Traditional tools tell you what went wrong (e.g., "Pod restarted 5 times"). k8s-inspector tells you why it happened and how to fix it (e.g., "OOMKilled because memory limit is too low. Click here to increase limit to 768Mi").

docs.title: Key Capabilities
docs.text:
• 🧠 AI-Powered Root Cause Analysis: Explains failures in plain English.
  • 💰 Intelligent Cost Optimization: Detects orphaned PVCs, unused LoadBalancers, and rightsizes deployments.
  • 🔮 Predictive Failure Detection: Uses ML to predict when resources will exhaust.
  • 🔐 Security Compliance: Checks against CIS benchmarks and NSA/CISA guidelines.
  • 🤖 Auto-Remediation Playbooks: Fixes issues automatically via YAML-based playbooks.

Next up: Check out the Getting Started guide to spin it up!

docs.title: Backend API Reference
docs.text:
k8s-inspector 2.0 is powered by a high-performance Python FastAPI backend that provides the core logic for the Next.js Dashboard.

This page provides an overview of the REST API and WebSocket capabilities.

docs.title: OpenAPI Documentation
docs.text: FastAPI automatically generates interactive OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation. When the backend is running locally, you can access it at: http://localhost:8000/docs

docs.title: 1. GET /api/v2/clusters
docs.text:
Returns a list of all registered Kubernetes clusters and their summary statistics. - Response: 

[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "prod-eu",
    "cluster_type": "kubernetes",
    "is_active": true,
    "health_score": 98.7,
    "node_count": 12,
    "pod_count": 340,
    "monthly_cost_estimate": 4200.0,
    "currency": "USD"
  }
]

docs.title: 2. POST /api/v2/clusters
docs.text:
Registers a new Kubernetes cluster with the inspector. - Body: 

{
  "name": "staging",
  "cluster_type": "kubernetes",
  "kubeconfig_secret": "secret_name_in_k8s"
}

docs.title: 3. GET /api/v2/insights
docs.text:
Retrieves generated insights from the Cost, Security, and ML Analyzers. - Filters: ?cluster_id=1&severity=critical&category=security - Response: 

[
  {
    "id": 42,
    "cluster_id": 1,
    "category": "security",
    "severity": "critical",
    "title": "Enforce Pod Security Standards",
    "recommendation": "Found 12 pod security violations (root execution).",
    "auto_fix_available": true
  }
]

docs.title: WebSocket Real-Time Telemetry
docs.text: The Next.js Dashboard communicates with the FastAPI backend over WebSockets to provide a live, real-time view of cluster anomalies, events, and metrics.

docs.title: ws://localhost:8000/ws/cluster/{cluster_id}
docs.text:
Connect to this endpoint to receive live updates.

Incoming Messages (from Backend): - Real-time metrics - Instant anomaly detections - Playbook execution logs

Outgoing Messages (from Dashboard): - Keep-alive ping - Trigger auto-remediation (Playbooks)

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: k8s-inspector 2.0 is built on a modern, decoupled architecture designed for scale and real-time observability.

docs.title: High-Level Design
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Next.js 14 Dashboard] <-->|REST & WebSockets| B(FastAPI Backend)
    B --> C[(PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB)]
    B --> D[(Redis Cache & Pub/Sub)]
    B <--> E[K8s API Server / Clusters]

    subgraph Analyzers
    B --> F[Cost Analyzer]
    B --> G[Security Analyzer]
    B --> H[ML Performance Engine]
    end

    subgraph Action
    B --> I[Playbook Engine]
    end

docs.title: 1. Frontend (Next.js 14)
docs.text:
• Framework: React / Next.js (App Router)
  • Styling: Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui
  • Real-time: WebSockets for live cluster telemetry

docs.title: 2. Backend (FastAPI)
docs.text:
• Framework: Python / FastAPI (Async)
  • Cluster Comm: Official kubernetes and openshift Python clients.
  • Concurrency: asyncio for high-throughput cluster polling.

docs.title: 3. Database Layer
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL: Stores relational data (Clusters, Insights, Playbooks).
  • TimescaleDB: Extension for high-performance time-series metric storage.
  • Redis: Session caching and WebSocket Pub/Sub broadcasting.

docs.title: 4. Engines
docs.text:
• Analyzers: Dedicated modules for Cost, Security, and ML/Performance.
  • Playbook Service: Parses YAML files and executes corrective Kubernetes API calls.

docs.title: Quick Start (Local Development)
docs.text:
The easiest way to try out k8s-inspector 2.0 is using Docker Compose. This will spin up the FastAPI backend, Next.js dashboard, PostgreSQL, and Redis.

git clone https://github.com/egkristi/k8s-inspector.git
cd k8s-inspector

# Start all services
docker-compose -f deploy/local/docker-compose.yml up -d

docs.title: Accessing the Services
docs.text:
• Dashboard: http://localhost:3000
  • API Documentation: http://localhost:8000/docs

docs.title: Production Deployment (Helm)
docs.text:
For production, deploy k8s-inspector directly into your Kubernetes cluster using our Helm chart.

# Add the Helm repository
helm repo add k8s-inspector https://charts.k8s-inspector.dev
helm repo update

# Install the chart
helm install k8s-inspector k8s-inspector/k8s-inspector \
  --namespace k8s-inspector \
  --create-namespace

docs.title: Helm Configuration
docs.text:
You can customize the installation by providing a values.yaml file:

frontend:
  ingress:
    enabled: true
    hosts:
      - host: dashboard.k8s.yourdomain.com
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix

ml:
  anomalyDetection:
    enabled: true

docs.title: Auto-Remediation Playbooks
docs.text:
One of the most powerful features of k8s-inspector is its ability to not just alert on issues, but fix them automatically. This is achieved through Playbooks.

Playbooks are declarative YAML files that map specific insights/alerts to a set of remediation actions.

docs.title: Playbook Structure
docs.text:
Every playbook must contain three main sections: trigger, remediation, and approval.

name: enforce-pss-baseline
description: Applies Pod Security Standard 'baseline' to a namespace
trigger: security_insight.rule == 'CIS-5.2.1'

remediation:
  - step: apply_pss_label
    action: kubectl label namespace {{ target.namespace }} pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline --overwrite

  - step: audit_pss
    action: kubectl label namespace {{ target.namespace }} pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted --overwrite

approval:
  required: true
  exceptions: [dev, staging]

docs.title: 1. Triggers
docs.text: The trigger field defines the condition that causes the playbook to run. It uses a simple expression language. * security_insight.rule == 'CIS-5.2.1' (Runs when a specific CIS rule is violated) * cost_insight.type == 'orphaned_pvc' (Runs when an unused volume is found) * anomaly.type == 'memory_leak' (Runs when the ML engine detects a memory leak)

docs.title: 2. Remediation Steps
docs.text:
This is an array of actions to perform. They execute sequentially. If one fails, the playbook execution stops. Available actions include: * kubectl patch ... * kubectl delete ... * kubectl label ... * slack_notify ...

We use Jinja2 templating ({{ target.namespace }}) to inject context from the insight directly into the command.

docs.title: 3. Approval Workflow
docs.text: Not all actions should happen automatically in production. * required: true: The remediation will wait in a "Pending" state in the Next.js Dashboard until a human clicks "Approve". * exceptions: [dev, staging]: You can configure environments where approval is not required, allowing full automation in lower environments.

docs.title: Creating Custom Playbooks
docs.text: You can easily add your own playbooks by dropping a .yaml file into the /playbooks directory of the application. The Playbook Engine automatically scans this folder on startup and registers new workflows.

docs.title: Cost Optimization & GreenOps
docs.text: The Cost Analyzer Engine continuously evaluates cluster resources to identify waste, over-provisioning, and environmental impact.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• GitOps Rightsizing: Instead of just alerting on over-provisioned deployments, automatically generate Pull Requests to fix requests and limits in your Helm or YAML files.
  • GreenOps via eBPF: Integrates with Kepler to track real power consumption (Watts) and carbon emissions (CO2) directly per pod, helping with ESG/CSRD reporting.
  • Agentic AI Cost Explainer: Ask the system in plain English why costs spiked (e.g., "Why did AWS cost jump 30%?"), powered by an opt-in LLM.
  • Bare-Metal Pricing: First-class support for on-premise clusters with hardware depreciation calculation and real-time electricity spot price integration.
  • Waste Detection: Identifies orphaned PVCs and unused LoadBalancers.

docs.title: ML & Performance Analyzer
docs.text: k8s-inspector goes beyond static CPU and Memory thresholds by utilizing machine learning algorithms to identify anomalies and predict resource exhaustion before it causes a cluster failure.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: The Performance Analyzer relies on historical metric data stored in our embedded TimescaleDB instance. It leverages scikit-learn and NumPy in the backend.

docs.title: 1. Anomaly Detection (Statistical Spikes)
docs.text: Instead of hardcoding a rule like "alert if CPU > 80%", the ML engine learns the standard operating behavior (baseline) of your cluster workloads over the past 7 to 30 days. - Isolation Forest / Z-score Models: It calculates the mean and standard deviation of your resources. If a metric spikes beyond (mean + 3*std_dev), an anomaly is flagged. - Contextual Spikes: A 90% CPU usage might be perfectly normal for a batch processing job, but highly unusual for an API gateway. The analyzer distinguishes between the two based on historical context.

docs.title: 2. Predictive Failure Detection (Trend Analysis)
docs.text: The engine monitors the rate of change for critical resources, such as memory usage, to predict when exhaustion will occur. - Memory Leaks: If memory usage for a specific pod increases linearly by > 1% per hour, the engine uses polynomial regression to calculate the "Time-to-OOMKill." - Capacity Planning: Predicts when a cluster node pool will run out of resources, enabling proactive scaling.

docs.title: Why ML-Driven?
docs.text: Static alerts lead to alert fatigue. If every pod that hits 85% memory triggers an alert, engineers start ignoring them. The Performance Analyzer ensures alerts are only triggered when the behavior is genuinely anomalous or trending towards a catastrophic failure.

docs.title: Technical Implementation
docs.text:
The ML analyzer executes inside the FastAPI backend (backend/app/analyzers/performance_analyzer.py).

  1. Data Ingestion: Regularly fetches CPU, Memory, and Network I/O from Prometheus/Metrics-Server.
  2. Time-Series Storage: Persists metrics to TimescaleDB.
  3. Inference Execution: Runs lightweight statistical models on the data in memory.
  4. Insight Generation: Triggers an Insight object if anomalies or trends are found.

These insights are displayed in the Next.js Dashboard and can automatically trigger scaling actions via Playbooks.

docs.title: Security Analyzer
docs.text: The Security Analyzer continuously monitors your Kubernetes clusters for misconfigurations and vulnerabilities. It evaluates the cluster against industry standards such as the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark and NSA/CISA Hardening Guidelines.

docs.title: 1. Pod Security Standards (PSS) Enforcement
docs.text: The analyzer detects pods and workloads running with elevated privileges. - Privileged Containers: Detects securityContext.privileged: true. - Root User Execution: Detects containers running as root instead of runAsNonRoot: true. - Host IPC/Network/PID: Alerts on containers sharing the host namespaces.

docs.title: 2. Network Isolation
docs.text: In Kubernetes, pods can communicate with each other freely by default. The Security Analyzer ensures your cluster follows the principle of least privilege for network traffic. - Default Deny-All: Verifies that every namespace contains a baseline NetworkPolicy denying unexpected traffic. - Unprotected Endpoints: Highlights exposed services lacking policy restrictions.

docs.title: 3. RBAC Auditing
docs.text: Role-Based Access Control misconfigurations are a common attack vector. - Wildcard Permissions: Detects ClusterRole or Role bindings that use "*" for verbs or resources (e.g., full cluster admin access). - Privilege Escalation: Highlights roles that grant the ability to create other roles or modify cluster role bindings.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The analyzer is a stateless Python module (backend/app/analyzers/security_analyzer.py) that executes via FastAPI background tasks.

  1. Telemetry Collection: It fetches definitions of running Pods, Namespaces, NetworkPolicies, and Roles from the target cluster.
  2. Evaluation Engine: It processes these objects against a set of predefined rules matching the CIS benchmarks.
  3. Scoring: The cluster is given a health score out of 100. Deductions are calculated based on severity:
  4. Critical: -10 points (e.g., Privileged containers, Wildcard RBAC)
  5. High: -5 points (e.g., Root execution, Missing NetworkPolicies)
  6. Medium/Low: -2/-1 points.

docs.title: Automated Remediation
docs.text: Many security insights generated by this analyzer can be fixed automatically using Playbooks. For example, if a namespace lacks a default-deny NetworkPolicy, the apply-default-deny-netpol playbook can be triggered to automatically inject it.
